data.title: Canadian Utilities: Catalysts Amid Modest Dividend Growth
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Since I last wrote about Canadian Utilities Limited (CU:CA) in May 2025, the company has posted a respectable total return of over 18%. After several years of sluggish growth, Canadian Utilities stock is benefiting due to several catalysts including the development of data centres in its operating jurisdiction and the advancement of some large electrical and gas transmission projects.

Operating in Canada, Mexico and Australia, Canadian Utilities is part of the ATCO Ltd. (ACO.X:CA) Family of companies, which owns a majority equity stake in the company. Canadian Utilities main operating utility group "ATCO Energy Systems" is the largest gas distributor in Alberta with 1.3M gas customers and 265,000 electrical customers.

Canadian Utilities is best known to investors as one of the preeminent dividend aristocrats in Canada. Although the company has slowed its pace of dividend growth in recent years, Canadian Utilities offers an unmatched dividend growth streak in Canada with 54 years of consecutive annual increases. With a 4.2% dividend yield, the utility continues to offer an steady source of cash payments to investors. The cash flow that underpins these dividends is backed by EBITDA generated from utility operations that are approximately 90% to 95% regulated.

Q3 2025 Results

For the first nine months of 2025, Canadian Utilities has delivered strong results, significantly improving on the company's performance over the first three quarters in 2024. Earnings attributable to shareholders increased from $316M in the year to September 30 2024 to $447M in the same period in 2025. This improvement is attributable to both operating segment improvements and losses on swap commodity contracts the dragged on earnings in 2024.

On the operations side, ATCO Energy Systems delivered adjusted earnings of $98 million in the quarter, $4 million higher year-over-year. The company's ATCO Australia division delivered adjusted earnings of $27 million during the quarter. This is $12 million or 80% higher than the same period last year.

Stock Performance and Valuation

Over the past 12 months, Canadian Utilities achieved total shareholder return of 33%, slightly ahead of the S&P/TSX Composite Index return of 32%. Over the same 12 month period to January 15 2026, the S&P 500 returned 16%. I don’t recall many periods where a boring utility has outperformed the broad indices.

Canadian Utilities is currently trading at 18.3x earnings, this level represents a 20% premium to the company’s 5-year average valuation of approximately 15.3x. Part of this rerating reflects broader sector trends as regulated utilities are seen as safe havens during market uncertainty. This multiple expansion also prices in near term growth prospects including some large projects coming on line in the next few years.

Favourable Operating Jurisdiction

In Q3 2025, Alberta’s population reached 5 million people. With a 2.25% growth rate, Alberta continues to lead population growth among Canada’s provinces and territories. Atco Energy Systems, Canadian Utilities main electric and gas operating company has an approved ROE of 8.97% for 2025. The company operates through seven distinct lines focusing on electric, gas , transmission and distribution. In the first 9 months of 2025 91% of the company’s revenue was derived from its Alberta operations. The Alberta Utilities Commission [AUC] is broadly seen as an accommodating regulator in the Canadian utility landscape. According to Fitch Ratings:

In Alberta, the AUC uses various regulatory mechanisms to bolster long-term financial stability. The AUC has a history of achieving multiyear rate increases in base rate proceedings, although with lower-than-average allowed return on equity [ROE] compared to U.S. peers. Fitch observes lower authorized ROEs across Canada than in comparable U.S. peers. However, CUL's Alberta utilities have a long track record of earning healthy returns, consistent with or exceeding U.S. peers.

Growth Plans

The company’s latest capital plan calls for investments of up to $6B in regulated utilities over the next three years. This capital growth is anticipated to drive compound annual growth of 5.4% on Canadian Utilities’ rate base.

The $2.9B Yellowhead Mainline natural gas pipeline is scheduled to begin construction as early as mid-2026 and start operations in late 2027. This project links the Northwest Alberta natural gas supply region to the Greater Edmonton area, home to Alberta’s industrial heartland. Reflecting the growing industrial demand for natural gas in this region, the Yellowhead pipeline project is already 90% contracted. On the company’s Q3 2025 earnings call, Canadian Utilities CEO, Robert Myles updated investors on the projects recent milestones:

We are pleased to announce the approval of the needs application from the Alberta Utilities Commission, or AUC, I'm also excited to announce that we filed the facilities application with the AUC earlier this week, which will provide detailed technical, environmental and consultation data required for construction approval.

In the fourth quarter, contracts for major equipment and steel pipe were awarded for this project and the remaining capacity has been marketed. The company also moved forward with its general rate application to the AUC seeking approval for Yellowhead pipeline revenue requirements of $381M, $489M and $680M for the years 2026, 2027 and 2028, respectively.

The Central East Transfer-Out project or CETO is a $280M project aimed at upgrading and strengthening the electrical transmission system in Central East Alberta. This project is targeted at addressing the congestion in the grid making energy transmission more efficient to Alberta’s major population centres. Canadian Utilities expects CETO to be in service in the first half of 2026.

Benefiting from the Alberta Data Centre Initiative

In December 2024, the province of Alberta, launched a plan outlining how the jurisdiction intends on attracting data centre investments in the province. Alberta’s AI data centre strategy, Powering the Future of Artificial Intelligence, argues that the province is an ideal location for data centre development based on its energy-only electricity market, low corporate tax rates and cold climate, which makes cooling more efficient. As of late 2024, there were 239 data centres in Canada, 22 of which are in Alberta. As one of the leading operators of Alberta's electrical transmission network, Canadian Utilities is well positioned to support this demand growth.

Dividend Streak Continues - Growth Rate Slows

Earlier in January, the company announced a quarterly dividend of $0.4623 per common share payable in March 2026. This modest 1% increase to the dividend is a continuation of the deceleration in dividend growth Canadian Utilities has been advancing in recent years. While the growth is slower, the record of dividend increases remains unappareled in Canada.

The recent dividend increase announcement marks the company’s 54th consecutive annual increase. Given the moderate payout ratio and slow dividend growth, investors can interpret this dividend growth profile as an indication of the company’s stable cash flows and near term opportunities to deploy capital to growth projects.

The modest dividend growth rate is also reflected in the company's relatively conservative payout ratio. Despite the ramping up of major capital projects in the last two years, Canadian Utilities been able to keep its dividend payout ratio stable. Canadian Utilities has a payout ratio of 55%, compared to Canadian-listed utility peers, Fortis Inc (FTS:CA) and Emera Inc (EMA:CA) with dividend payout ratios of 71% and 77% respectively.

At current levels, the dividend yield is 4.2%, approximately 19% lower than the company's 5-year average yield. This decrease in yield reflects the strong share price performance in 2025.

Risk Analysis

Canadian Utilities is domestically focused and regionally regulated. The vast majority of revenue is generated in the province of Alberta. These characteristics help to insulate the stock from the geopolitical risk factors that weigh on sectors more affected by international trade and investment.

Despite the Alberta Utilities Commission broadly being considered to govern a constructive regulatory framework for utility operators in Alberta, the regulator recently required Canadian Utilities to refund $71M to customers for failing to demonstrate cost savings to customers under the company’s performance commitments.

Interest rate risk is another key risk that all utility operators contend with. Not only do higher interest rates raise the cost of capital for utility operators with high leverage, higher available interest rates make the stock’s dividend yield less attractive relative to fixed income alternatives.

Relative to peers, Canadian Utilities has slightly elevated leverage with 6.1x FFO leverage. This compares to approximately 5.9x for Fortis Inc. (FTS:CA) and 5.8-5.9x for Emera Inc (EME:CA). As of Q2 2025 Canadian Utilities and its subsidiaries have access to approximately $3.6B in committed credit facilities with approximately $2.4B in available liquidity. In addition to sufficient liquidity, Canadian Utilities has been successful in raising cash to finance its capital program. According to CFO Katie Patrick:

On September 8, Canadian Utilities Limited announced a $750 million transaction of hybrid notes at a fixed rate of 5.45%. And on September 11, CU Inc. announced a $370 million transaction of debentures at a rate of 4.787%. I am proud to say that these offerings had significant interest from the investment community at approximately we're 3x oversubscribed across a strong pool of buyers.

This is a positive indication that the company will be able to raise the capital required to fund the Yellowhead project with a combination of 63% regulated debt and 37% regulated equity.

Investor Takeaways

Canadian Utilities is advancing major capital projects that will support a growing rate base. This highly regulated utility operates in a constructive regulatory environment and is poised to benefit from continued population growth and growing demand for residential, industrial and data centre power needs in Alberta. While dividend growth has slowed to token levels, the current dividend yielding 4.2% remains well covered.

With the strong share price performance valuation above historical levels, I have recently taken some profits in Canadian Utilities. I continue to hold a position and plan to reinvest dividends. With the proceeds from the recent trimming of my position, I added to peer utility - Fortis. While I still like Canadian Utilities for its Alberta exposure and regulated business model, I prefer Fortis at this time for its superior dividend growth profile.

data.title: Hyatt: There Are Definitely Stronger Options In The Segment
data.description:
Investment Thesis

When I stayed at a Hyatt Hotels Corporation (H), I remember my impressions: The atmosphere, the service, the luxury. Given the emphasis on the premium segment and the experience I gained researching other hotel stocks, I expected to see a positive assessment for my investment portfolio from Hyatt Hotels Corporation shares. Instead, the output of my quantitative analysis were somewhat disappointing, resulting in H stocks receiving a negative sell rating.

Thesis Explanation

I've been using a quantitative approach to stock analysis for a long time. I've developed my own stock evaluation system, which consists of eight parameters. If the overall rating exceeds 70%, the stock can be added to a long-term portfolio. If the rating is below 50%—that is, the financial performance, financial position, and growth factor scores are only half-perfect or worse—I try to avoid such stocks.

My approach showed that Hyatt is only strong in the growth area, while profitability and financial position are rather poor. Since growth metrics dominate my weighted rating, positive double-digit growth figures could boost the overall value, but beyond that, I found nothing compelling in the financials or the company. The overall rating is less than 50% of the benchmark for an outstanding stock.

Hyatt Is Just Moving To An Asset-Light Model

Unlike Hilton (HLT), Hyatt began its transition to a franchising model 10 years later. This is the main fundamental reason for the lag on financials, in my opinion. Recently, the company has been actively selling assets, while remaining a hotel operator under a long-term management agreement. According to 2024 reports, the company received more than $1.6 billion by selling assets such as Hyatt Regency Aruba Resort Spa & Casino, Hyatt Regency Orlando and others.

As of Q3 2025, the owned segment's share ~37% of all direct revenue, while Hilton's share is even lower (~24%).

Despite a solid gross profit margin of 42%, the quarter was very weak, with a loss of $50 million. This resulted in net income TTM being negative for the first time since COVID. This loss is largely due to two factors.

First, over the last four quarters, no gains from sales that would be considered non-recurring in financial analysis were recorded. And once the effect of one-time sales disappeared, the true state of the income statement was revealed.

Secondly, I want to note the high debt burden. Average debt over the past 12 months, according to my calculations, exceeded $5.3 billion, with shareholders` equity just over $3.5 billion for the same period. This significant debt load resulted in $282 million of the $485 million operating income TTM (or 58%) being spent on debt servicing. That's too much.

Due to the net loss, neither ROE nor ROIC nor earnings quality are available for analysis, so in my opinion, the company's financial performance and financial position are among the worst in the sector and are not sufficient for including the stock in a long-term investment portfolio.

Ascending Phase Of The Growing Cycle

While everything seems really dire for now, there are some positive aspects.

Taking into account the sale of some hotels and the transition to a management model, as well as the industry-wide growth cycle and organic room growth, the three-year EPS CAGR was a significant 38%. It's rare for this figure to be higher than the five-year cycle one (~20%).

Given the ongoing transition to an asset-light model, we can expect margin growth and an expansion of the company's premium portfolio. Analysts expect EPS to approach $4.37 by December 2027, representing a CAGR of more than 70%.

But I should notice that the asset-light model is more effective in larger chains, where fixed G&A costs are spread over a larger volume. Currently, Hyatt is much smaller than Hilton (~300k rooms versus ~1.5m). That's why I consider these forecasts to be at least optimistic.

The second important point: When we talk about growth rates, it's important to remember that 2020 and 2021 were very challenging for the industry, and the company suffered significant losses. Therefore, using three- and five-year metrics, we're now facing a low base effect.

Sales of some hotels also complicate fair valuations. For example, on Dec. 30, it was announced that Hyatt completed a $2 billion sale of Playa's owned real estate portfolio to Tortuga. I'm not sure how exactly this deal will be reflected in the reports and what impact it will have on quarterly and annual results. But at a minimum, it will definitely distort net operating efficiency.

Bottom Line

To summarize, we're seeing an interesting situation. I've settled on a sell rating, but this is not a recommendation for active sell or short. It's more of a cautionary tale and my own tactic of avoiding H stock. Why?

Because I'm currently observing a transitional phase in the business, but not like SAP, for example (I wrote about this here). Margins are declining, debt is weighing heavily, and the current financial position remains at least average.

Against this background, all-time highs seem unjustified.

On the other hand, there are decent profit forecasts and clear benefits from the transition to franchising and management with a focus on the premium segment.

The main risk of my thesis is a positive scenario of transition to a lightweight model, a rapid recovery of margins, and, as a result, high net profit. Then, both debt and profitability metrics will return to normal, and my rating will be unjustified post-factum.

Hyatt is more closely tied to luxury and lifestyle brands than any other US hotel chain. This segment is expected to grow faster than average in the coming years. Even with economic fluctuations, premium leisure and high-end business travel remain resilient. Barring a recession, Hyatt could post unexpectedly strong quarters.

But I still suggest holding off on buying shares, as buying solely on the prospects in an industry like the hotel business contradicts my cautious strategy for building a long-term investment portfolio. Pandemics or military conflicts can set back business development by years, and currency fluctuations are a daily risk factor for shareholders in such a sector. Therefore, for H stock to be a strong candidate, I'd like to see a clear signal of success in the transition to an asset-light model. While I may risk buying the stock higher and higher, I want to be confident I'm buying an outstanding business. I'm nowhere near that certainty right now.

data.title: Impax Large Cap Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance

The US Large Cap portfolio’s underperformance versus the S&P 500 Index was primarily attributed to challenging security selection across key technology-related holdings. Questions around investments returns on capital expenditures related to AI, and AI’s potentially negative impact on software as a service, pressured stocks such as ServiceNow (NOW), Tyler Technologies (TYL), Oracle (ORCL), Microsoft (MSFT) and Netflix (NFLX). The team retains strong conviction in these companies and the durability of their competitive advantages. Selection was also weak in Industrials and Materials as investors gravitated towards more speculative, risky securities and away from higher quality companies with proven track records. Within Consumer Discretionary there was a clear bifurcation in fundamentals as the higher- income consumer continues to be resilient while the lower-income consumer is showing weaker spending trends. The portfolio’s exposure to strong brands and resilient end markets drove positive security selection within the sector. Selection in Real Estate was also a tailwind as the portfolio’s exposure to the strong secular opportunity related to senior living was rewarded as Ventas reported continued occupancy growth and higher rental income. Regional bank Citizens Financial Group was another strong performer, as the stock benefited from a steepening yield curve and strong fundamentals.

Market overview

Equity markets, as measured by the S&P 500 Index, ended the quarter modestly higher. The period was marked by bouts of volatility, driven by concerns over elevated valuations and uncertainty surrounding returns and financing for large-scale data center investments. These factors prompted profit-taking in certain mega-cap technology and AI-related companies, although the market overall remained resilient. Market leadership shifted towards Health Care, which rallied following the Trump administration’s agreement with major pharmaceutical firms to reduce Medicaid drug prices. Pro-cyclical sectors also outperformed, with Materials buoyed by commodity strength, and Financials expected to benefit from looser monetary conditions. Small caps were also positive reaching new highs, as investors seek out attractive valuations.

Softer labor market data and signs of consumer weakness in residential housing led the Federal Reserve (Fed) to deliver two additional interest rate cuts during the quarter, despite persistent inflationary pressures. While some economic indicators softened, US Q3 GDP growth surprised to the upside, and corporate earnings remained robust. A new Fed Chair in 2026 is likely to maintain a ‘dovish’ stance, with further rate cuts highly likely throughout the year.

As we enter 2026, our macroeconomic outlook is one of cautious optimism. Falling interest rates and rising government spending should provide support for risk assets. However, narrow drivers of economic growth and unsustainable fiscal trajectories remain areas of concern. Within equities, we continue to take a critical view of certain valuations and expect investors to refocus on fundamentals and seek opportunities in companies whose potential has been overlooked. Our investment approach continues to emphasize businesses with strong growth drivers, sound management and attractive valuations, enabling us to build well-diversified portfolios that are differentiated from the broader market.

Sector Attribution

Only two sectors outperformed the index during the quarter: Health Care, which benefited from its defensive characteristics and attractive valuations that drew inflows during periods of market uncertainty; and Communication Services, which was supported by Google’s AI breakthroughs and potential acquisition activity involving Warner Brothers Discovery. Financials delivered mixed results, with capital markets outperforming traditional lenders after the December rate cut. Information Technology (IT) experienced challenges in December due to ongoing concerns about elevated AI-related capital expenditures, while Industrials gains were concentrated primarily in segments like logistics, aerospace and defense. Consumer Discretionary performance was divided, with areas supporting higher income consumers showing some resilience. Consumer Staples underperformed as questions around organic volume growth for many companies in the sector weighed on investor sentiment. Materials were driven by metals and mining equities, reflecting sustained strength in industrial commodity prices. Real Estate, typically sensitive to interest rates, might have been expected to benefit from easing rate expectations but continued to face headwinds related to long-term financing concerns. Utilities experienced profit-taking after electric utility stocks delivered strong returns over the calendar year.

The portfolios sector tilts were a marginal detractor in the period, with the overweight to Health Care, and underweights to Consumer Staples and IT contributing positively. The underweight to Communication Services was the largest detractor followed by the overweight allocations in Real Estate and Industrials.

Contributors

Danaher (DHR) (Health Care, Life Sciences Tools & Services) delivered strong Q4 performance driven by robust demand for bioprocessing and diagnostics products, supported by continued strength in pharmaceutical and biotechnology end-markets. Margin expansion from cost efficiencies and successful integration of recent acquisitions further boosted earnings, reinforcing investor confidence.

Meta Platforms (META) (Communication Services, Interactive Media & Services), which is not held in the portfolio, declined due to weaker advertising revenue growth and rising expenses tied to its continued investment in AI infrastructure and the metaverse, which pressured margins. Additionally, cautious guidance for 2026 and concerns about slowing user engagement in key markets contributed to investor pessimism and a broad tech sector pullback.

Agilent Technologies (A) (Health Care, Life Sciences Tools & Services) delivered solid Q4 2025 performance supported by strong demand in pharmaceutical and biotechnology end-markets, particularly for analytical instruments and consumables. Operational efficiencies and disciplined cost control helped offset macroeconomic headwinds, driving margin stability and reinforcing investor confidence.

Detractors

Alphabet Class C (GOOG) (Communication Services, Interactive Media & Services), which is not held in the portfolio as the investment team owns the Class A shares, was one of the top performers in the period. The rising share price was driven by robust advertising revenue growth, particularly from YouTube and Search, as digital advertising spending rebounded during the holiday season. Cloud revenues saw double-digit growth and margin improvement, while cost optimization initiatives boosted profitability, leading to positive investor sentiment.

Oracle (ORCL) (Information Technology, Systems Software) was decidedly weaker in the quarter, as investors weighed strong stock performance in the third quarter alongside concerns over rising competition in cloud infrastructure and margin pressure from heavy AI-related capital expenditures.

Uber Technologies (UBER) (Industrials, Passenger Ground Transportation) was down due to weaker-than-expected ride-hailing demand in key markets and rising regulatory scrutiny over driver classification, which raised cost concerns. Additionally, higher insurance and compliance expenses pressured margins, while investor sentiment cooled amid slower growth in delivery services.

Outlook

Looking forward, the outlook for US equities is marked by cautious optimism. The Federal Reserve’s policy decisions are under close scrutiny, with a growing consensus among market participants that additional interest rate cuts may be forthcoming by mid-year should inflation continue its downward trajectory. While the labor market remains relatively robust, early signs of normalization are emerging, which could help alleviate wage-driven inflation and bolster corporate profit margins. Against this backdrop, we are monitoring several key forces shaping the economic landscape: technological innovation, shifting consumer preferences, broader societal changes, and evolving regulation and policy. The team believes companies that have consistently delivered solid earnings growth, yet remain undervalued relative to peers, are well placed to outperform as investor focus returns to fundamentals and valuation.

The investment team believes the market continues to underestimate the growth opportunities coming from long-term secular trends. While the market’s recent focus has been narrowly focused on artificial intelligence and interest rate expectations, the team sees very attractive opportunities and valuations that significantly discount the longer-term growth rate for companies addressing these secular trends. The team’s focus remains on identifying companies with secular tailwinds, durable competitive advantages and attractive valuations. Looking into 2026, the team believes the portfolio is very well positioned to capitalize on secular opportunities while holding well-managed companies with the proven track records and strong balance sheets to provide ballast in volatile market environments.

Impax Large Cap Fund is a core equities strategy focused on US large cap companies with strong prospects and attractive valuations.

data.title: AGNC: Strong Investment Setup In 2026
data.description:
AGNC Investment Corp. (AGNC) managed to grow its portfolio of MBS in the fourth quarter and delivered high single-digit growth in its tangible book value compared to the year-earlier period. If federal fund rates head lower in 2026, the mortgage REIT could be set to benefit from a strong revaluation catalyst for its mortgage-backed securities portfolio, in my opinion.

AGNC continued to grow its net interest income rapidly in the fourth quarter (10%+), which highlights robust core investment profitability. While shares are not cheap, trading at a premium of 34% to GAAP book value, the market clearly expects AGNC to perform well this year. With more rate cuts on the horizon and inflation being less of a threat than last year, AGNC maintains a strong investment setup for investors looking for income and book value growth.

Previous Rating

AGNC was a Strong Buy for me previously (October 2025) -- Strong NII, BV Growth, Upside -- mainly because of the potential for a mortgage-backed securities repricing in a falling-rate world. Mortgage-backed securities, or MBS, are bond-like instruments, meaning they can be expected to do well for institutional investors if the Federal Reserve lowers interest rates. I like AGNC as a revaluation play with regard to its mortgage-backed securities portfolio, and I see a year of strong book value growth for the investment firm, especially if the Federal Reserve were to accelerate its rate cut trajectory in 2026.

Strong NII Gains in the Fourth Quarter

AGNC’s investment portfolio consists chiefly of Agency mortgage-backed securities that have the implicit guarantee of the U.S. government against credit losses, meaning investors are protected from borrower default risk. The mortgage REIT owned $81.8B in mortgage-backed securities at the end of the fourth quarter, showing 6.2% quarter-over-quarter growth. Because mortgage-backed securities increase in value during times of falling interest rates, they are attractive investments to buy when interest rates can be expected to head lower.

AGNC did not only report strong growth in its portfolio (and book) value, but also delivered robust results for the fourth quarter in terms of actual investment performance: the mortgage REIT’s interest income grew 10.3% year-over-year to $944M while interest expenses declined.

As a result, AGNC’s net interest income grew 79% year-over-year, and the REIT benefited from 8X faster growth in net interest income compared to its interest income. The implication is that ANGC’s investment business is set for stronger profitability in a falling-rate environment, which is exactly what I expect in 2026: after the Federal Reserve lowered interest rates by 25 basis points in December, more rate cuts are highly likely this year. I expect the Federal Reserve to cut interest rates at least 2-3 times in 2026, potentially setting AGNC up for even stronger net interest income growth in the quarters ahead.

Due to strong NII performance and higher net interest income, the mortgage REIT's economic return bounced back in the last two quarters as well: the economic return surged to 11.6% in the fourth quarter, the highest level since Q4 '23.

AGNC’s Valuation and Book Value Growth

Shares of AGNC are currently priced at a 34% premium to book value, which compares to a 24% premium for shares of Annaly Capital Management (NLY), which has not yet reported results for its fourth quarter. Both mortgage REITs effectively trade like bonds in the stock market as their underlying mortgage investments react inversely to changes in interest rates. Therefore, mortgage-backed securities are uniquely suitable investments for investors that expect falling interest rates.

On the opposite end of the mortgage asset investment spectrum are mortgage servicing rights, which increase in value during rising-rate periods, largely because the underlying fee stream becomes more valuable over time. This means that investors should invest in mortgage servicing rights when federal fund rates rise and buy mortgage-backed securities when rates are about to fall.

While shares of AGNC now trade at a significant premium to book value -- the historical P/B ratio in the last three years is just 1.06X -- the prospect of falling interest rates in 2026 supports an investment, in my opinion. I also recommend Annaly Capital, as the mortgage REIT platform has seen improving core earnings strength and last year raised its dividend as well: 1.04X Coverage, Strong BV Growth, Cheap.

In terms of book value, AGNC has seen a robust rebound in its trajectory in the last two quarters, highlighting that the investment setup for mortgage REITs has in fact greatly improved. AGNC's net book value increased to $8.88 per share in the fourth quarter, showing 7.2% Q/Q growth. With more rate cuts on the horizon this year, I believe AGNC and Annaly Capital could both see significant (~10%) growth in their book values in 2026.

Risks With AGNC

The biggest risk for the mortgage REIT is that the Federal Reserve takes its time with cutting interest rates in 2026, which would delay an important repricing catalyst for the company’s MBS portfolio. What would get me to potentially change my mind about AGNC is if the Federal Reserve were to start hiking federal fund rates, which currently runs counter to consensus expectations. This could happen in an accelerating U.S. economy or if inflation were to make a comeback. As I explained here, the odds are in favor of strong U.S. GDP growth in the fourth quarter, which could potentially affect the Fed's rate-cutting timeline.

Final Thoughts

AGNC submitted a decent earnings report card for the fourth quarter that highlighted strong net interest income growth, higher net interest spreads, and high single-digit book value growth amid a favorable investment environment for mortgage-backed securities.

Both AGNC and Annaly Capital are directional plays on falling interest rates in 2026, and I would expect a gradual improvement in the investment environment this year. While shares of AGNC now trade at a significant premium to book value, I believe the market has adjusted its expectations lately, especially after the Federal Reserve lowered interest rates by 25 basis points in December.

With more federal fund rate cuts in the cards this year, AGNC has a strong investment setup and could drive its book value significantly higher on the back of a repricing of its rate-sensitive mortgage-backed securities.

data.title: National Vision Holdings: I Don't See Market-Beating Returns Now (Downgrade)
data.description:
Back in December 2023, I decided to dig into National Vision Holdings (EYE), a rather interesting company that, at the time, had been experiencing some pain because of the termination of its partnership with Walmart (WMT). At that time, the company was generating significant revenue from that arrangement. But because of how that relationship was structured, the overall impact on the bottom line was relatively minimal, or I guess I should say outsized in a way that did not severely affect the company. Despite this issue that the company experienced, I viewed the picture in a bullish light. Management had a plan for future growth, and shares were trading on the cheap. At the end of the day, I had no choice but to rate the company a "Buy."

The trajectory since then has been quite positive. Even though we have seen some volatility, revenue, profits, and cash flows have all generally trended higher. The number of locations that the business has in operation increased, and future growth looks promising. The stock price has reflected this, with shares rising 48.9%. However, it is worth noting that this still puts the company slightly behind the S&P 500, which has generated a return for investors of 51.1% over that same period. This is the kind of performance relative to the market that is more in line with a "Hold" rating. And even though the company has done well, shares are more expensive now than they were back then from a valuation standpoint. In light of this, I do believe that downgrading the stock to a "Hold" is the appropriate course of action.

I see limited upside

Whenever I go a long time without analyzing a company, I'd like to take a step back and look at its overall business model. This helps to re-familiarize myself with the enterprise. Operationally speaking, National Vision Holdings is an interesting business. The company operates as one of the largest optical retailers in the United States. They do this through a few different brands. They own two of these, the first being America’s Best Contacts and Eyeglasses, and the second being Eyeglass World. However, the company also has what it calls host brands. These are brands that it essentially manages. They include Vista Optical locations that are in certain Fred Meyer stores, as well as Vista Optical locations that are situated on military bases.

Management has long promoted the company as a key player in the value segment. This is for the budget-constrained consumer, which should actually make it quite appealing in the current economic climate when consumer confidence is near all-time lows. For instance, at its America’s Best locations, it offers up two pairs of eyeglasses, as well as an eye exam, all for $89.95. Two pairs of eyeglasses can be purchased for $99 at its Eyeglass World locations. To be honest with you, these numbers are shockingly low. The place that I go for glasses costs me between $200 and $400, depending on where I get them. So this does mean real value for shareholders relative to what other options are available.

Conceptually, this is a large market opportunity for the company. According to management, the optical retail market in the United States was worth about $70 billion as of 2024. However, it is a space that's continuing to grow. By 2030, they estimate that it will be worth $85 billion or more. And currently, National Vision Holdings operates as the third-largest player out there. However, that still means that it has a market share of only about 2.6%, which means that this is a highly fragmented industry that offers the opportunity for significant growth by acquisition or by muscling out smaller competitors that are unable to compete with low costs. And that's where this business truly shines.

Long term, the company sees multiple growth vectors that it can tackle. This includes underdeveloped customers, which would be those that fall under managed care, those that are outside prescriptions, and the like. It includes the underdeveloped products category, such as frame premiumization, anti-reflective offerings, transitions, progressive lenses, and the like. It includes improving the customer and patient experience through the use of data-driven segmentation, the integration of e-commerce and customer relationship management activities, and more. And it also involves new store growth that targets new markets and that invests in new formats.

Management is confident that it can continue to play in the value category while also pushing for higher prices. For instance, it believes that, through product innovation and price increases, it can raise product-only revenue by between 1.5 and 3 times as time goes on. That is significant in and of itself. But of course, there is a fine line to walk here, since consumers are budget constrained and price sensitive to things like this. We already see, for instance, that while its share of lower-income customers is comparable with the share of those who are low income in the United States, it does cater to a larger share of middle-income individuals. I actually do find this to be rather interesting considering that the value play should apply more to those who are lower income. But that suggests to me that the ability to hike prices, unless doing so purposely targets the higher-income individuals that are underrepresented in the company's revenue stream, could be limited.

Over the last few years now, management has done a fine job of capitalizing on the opportunities that exist. This has shown up in the form of revenue. In the chart above, you can see financial results for the 2022 through 2024 fiscal years. Revenue expanded from $1.64 billion to $1.82 billion. This growth has come about pretty much entirely by its America’s Best brand, with revenue shooting up from $1.37 billion to $1.56 billion. This can be chalked up to an increase in the number of locations from 905 to 1,036.

By comparison, other areas for the company have either stagnated or declined. In the chart below, you can see overall location count for the company, including by vertical. This includes results through the first nine months of 2025 relative to the same time of 2024 as well.

We continued to see growth associated with America’s Best, with an increase in store count to 1,049 locations. That's up from the 1,019 locations that the company had a year earlier. Eyeglass World, meanwhile, has seen a continual decline in location count since 2023, while the other segments for the company have either flatlined, or in the case of its Fred Meyer locations, dropped lately.

Profitability for the company has been rather mixed, as one of the previous charts illustrates. But the good news is that, for 2025, things are looking up. As the chart above illustrates, revenue expanded to $1.48 billion during the first nine months of 2025. That's up from the $1.39 billion reported the previous year. Overall profitability surged, while cash flows rose nicely. During this period, the company benefited significantly from comparable store sales growth. In fact, when it came to the business as a whole, this was 5.7%, with America’s Best rising a hefty 6.7% year over year.

Management attributed this to higher average ticket prices at a time when customer traffic was essentially flat. This is interesting to me, but not necessarily surprising. When you are one of the cheapest players out there, or perhaps the cheapest, and you operate in a space where what you provide is essentially needed by a large chunk of the population, you can hike prices so long as your price is still comparable to or lower than the competition. This would obviously have a huge positive impact on profitability as well, which is precisely what we have seen.

When it comes to the 2025 fiscal year in its entirety, management projected revenue of between $1.97 billion and $1.99 billion. At the midpoint, we would be looking at revenue of $1.98 billion, which would represent an 8.5% increase year over year. The firm is on track, based on the results achieved for the first nine months of the year, to generate EBITDA of around $182.4 million and adjusted operating cash flow of $148.3 million. Using these estimates, as well as the results that the company achieved in 2024, we can see in the chart above how shares of the business are currently priced. These are not bad multiples by any means, but they do put the business well in the fair value category as I describe it. They are certainly more expensive than they were when I last wrote about the business. In that chart, however, I did show that, compared to competitor Warby Parker (WRBY), which is admittedly not the perfect comparable given its e-commerce-oriented business model and its partnership with Google parent Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) aimed at providing smart glasses, shares are considerably cheaper. But I do chalk that up more to business model disparities than an indicator of actual value that investors can capitalize on.

Long term, we should expect continued growth from the company. For years, management had been growing at a rate of about 60 locations per year. This occurred between 2019 and 2023. But for the 2024 through 2027 years, it has been targeting slower pace of growth, with an average of about 30 stores per year. However, between the investments the company is making in innovation, changes in its competitive position through pricing, and the like, it expects to resume the more accelerated store growth path of 60 locations per year between 2028 and 2030. The fact that management has confidence in this is certainly encouraging, though we will have to wait and see if this expansion materializes.

Takeaway

Based on all the data provided, I would argue that National Vision Holdings is an interesting prospect. But it's not one that I find to be the most compelling. The company should continue to grow nicely, though shares are not as cheap as I would like them to be. It is in light of this valuation that, even though I like the business from a growth perspective, I believe that downgrading it to a "Hold" makes sense.

data.title: SLJY: 18% Target Income Silver Covered Call ETF To Buy
data.description:
The Amplify SILJ Covered Call ETF (SLJY) is a relatively new (established August 2025) covered call ETF with a focus on silver, or, more specifically, junior silver mining companies, which exhibit a fairly strong correlation with silver (SLV) itself.

As most Amplify ETF products, SLJY is also rather unique, embodying certain properties that are not that widely used among other well-established covered call ETF names such as the NEOS NASDAQ-100 High Income ETF (QQQI) and the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ).

The same could be said if we compare SLJY to some other silver covered call ETF alternatives: the UBS ETRACS Silver Shares Covered Call ETN (SLVO) and the Kurv Silver Enhanced Income ETF (KSLV).

If we put this on a chart - comparing SLJY to its closest peers, silver and silver miners (SIL) - we will immediately arrive at some fundamental observations:

First, silver and silver miners are strongly correlated (i.e., experience the same rate of change patterns), but the magnitude of these movements can differ significantly.

Second, even though SIL has surged and SLJY carries a covered call factor, the realized returns have been almost identical, which sends a message that the embedded opportunity cost that sits within SLJY's covered call wrapper is fairly limited.

I think that this is the right segway to transition the next chapter in which I elaborate more on SLJY's offering and how (why) it could be used for strategic portfolio income enhancement.

Thesis

Let's start with the big picture.

As the chart below shows, silver miners and silver are correlated, but with certain periods in which more pronounced divergences can happen:

All in all, both of these asset classes are subject to the same demand drivers:

  • Technology and AI (electronic components for data centers).
  • Inflation protection and shield against dollar decline (store of value).
  • Demand from China.
  • Solar energy and EVs (critical component to enable these assets).

However, there are some structural differences. As opposed to pure-play silver holding, silver miners come with equity risk, which automatically brings to the equation such variables as financial risk (balance sheet management), execution risk and the ability to convert the current megatrend into real/tangible cash flows. Plus, an argument could be made that equity holdings are not as solid of a hedge against dollar depreciation and inflation as the physical metal.

Having said that, in the long-run these two assets tend to mean-revert, which given the current situation provides an opportunity to transform the disconnect into a real and potentially lucrative investment play - i.e., buying silver miners and expecting an outperformance from the mean-reversion process.

Unless one has a very high conviction view on physical silver and it is a bullish one, then I don't see a meaningful counterargument to a silver miner play. As for a value and income investor, the current divergence between the two asset groups actually renders SIL more interesting for me to consider integrating in the portfolio.

Now, and here comes SLJY's part and why it is, in my view, a very solid vehicle to implement that trade in an income-friendly (actually, income rich) manner.

As I briefly mentioned in the intro section, SLJY is a covered call ETF, which carries silver miners in its underlying portfolio. What it means is that SLJY invests directly in silver miners (has around 60 holdings, including calls) and then sells call options against these individual names (not on an index level).

The individual stock covered call writing generally allows it to capture higher volatility (and thus higher option income premium) relative to an index-level overwrite strategy. FEPI is a great example of that, offering a yield of around 25%, while comparable AI-focused covered call ETFs yield between 12% to 15%.

Speaking of SLJY's covered calls, it is also important to appreciate that we are talking about out-of-the-money contracts, which leave decent upside potential in the system. This is also the main reason SLJY has not significantly underperformed the booming SIL index. Usually, the key disadvantage of covered call strategies is that they cap the upside, which in the upwards trending markets can introduce a sizeable opportunity cost (i.e., not being able to profit from the share/asset price appreciation).

For example, one of the largest option contracts that SLJY currently has in its portfolio is related to its Hecla Mining (HL) stake. The call is almost 20% out of the money, which leaves ample upside for SLJY to participate in.

On top of this well-managed opportunity cost (or risk), SLJY has a strategy in place that aims to achieve an annualized option income of 18%:

By incorporating a tactical OTM covered call options writing strategy, the fund seeks to monetize volatility to generate 18% annualized option premium income while also offering the upside potential of junior silver mining companies

It means that SLJY calibrates an option overwrite portion and the level of out of the moneyness so that it can capture 18% of option income. If we annualize the two most recent distributions, then we would get a forward or implied dividend yield of ~15%. It is a bit lower than the stated target, but given the increasing distribution trajectory and SLJY active management (e.g., making decisions upon call option contract expiries how far to go in the OTM and overwrite areas), it would be only reasonable to assume that on a go forward basis the amount of income collected from SLJY should correspond to the ~18% target level.

So far, the story sounds quite attractive:

  • Participating in a silver bull run.
  • Opening an investment play with a silver miner vs. physical silver mean-reversion.
  • Collecting 18% in passive income streams.

The thing that could turn the entire case upside down is obviously a major correction, which is not atypical in the commodity and miner space.

If the silver market plunges, the chances will be high that SIL and SLJY would follow in the same direction. While SLJY would be better protected than the index due to the collected option premiums, but that would not be enough to avoid the risk of locking lower option contract values on a depreciated asset base. In that moment, the annual income stream level would likely remain the same (18% of the asset base), but the absolute level of income would come in much lower simply because of the smaller denominators (i.e., lower portfolio value).

The bottom line

SLJY presents an attractive opportunity for income investors to integrate silver-linked exposure into their portfolios, while still ticking the "yield-bearing asset" box, which would not be possible in a pure-play silver investment case or even if that trade was expressed via the silver miner index, which yields less than 1%.

The 18% income component that is fueled by OTM option income makes SLJY even more appealing - a real weapon that can enhance the overall portfolio income.

SLJY is certainly a covered call ETF to consider for income investors, who are bullish on silver and can stomach potentially violent downside volatility, which tends to happen in the commodity space. The downside volaltility would not only affect the price level, but also decrease the income stream accordingly.

However, if consistent and stable passive income is a factor that cannot be sacrificed, then SLJY might not be the right choice.

data.title: Madison Mid Cap Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
For the full year, the Russell Midcap Index returned 10.6%, capping off an exceptionally strong three year run, amounting to an annual increase of over 14%. Similar to recent years, in 2025, market leadership favored more volatile and speculative companies. This was most pronounced in the strong rebound off of the Russell Midcap Index’s low on April 8th, from which areas like unprofitable technology companies, bitcoin exposed equities, and quantum computing businesses advanced over 100%. There were also several other more speculative areas of the market that advanced meaningfully during the year. This type of market environment typically represents a headwind for our portfolio, given our focus on high-quality, highly profitable, durable businesses, which we believe sell for reasonable prices. Historically, this has been a strong foundation for attractive long-term investment performance, and we remain committed to this discipline. In fact, we’re excited by the opportunities that the market presented in our types of businesses during the year and we were active in taking advantage of them.

Portfolio Performance

The Madison Mid Cap Fund (GTSGX) decreased 1.2% in the fourth quarter of 2025, compared to the 0.2% increase in the Russell Midcap Index.

The top five contributors for the quarter were Ross Stores (ROST), MKS (MKSI), Waters (WAT), PACCAR (PCAR), and Amphenol (APH). Ross Stores reported very strong sales results in the quarter and management offered an optimistic outlook for the rest of the year. The company is benefiting from its strategy to offer even better value, by further emphasizing familiar brands at low prices. Sales are up across all categories and more consumers are visiting Ross stores. MKS shares advanced nicely during the quarter as the business continues to benefit from end markets picking up. Specifically, their Electronics and Packaging business is showing accelerating growth, partly due to AI-related applications. Waters’ 3Q results reflected continued strong organic growth in both its instruments and consumables businesses as well as across all geographies.

PACCAR’s current results continued to be pressured by the depressed end market environment, but tariff relief, environmental regulations, and a cyclical end market recovery point to results improving from here. Amphenol’s IT/Datacom business continues to produce spectacular organic growth on the back of Al-related data center spending, which is driving up the earnings and share price of the stock.

The bottom five detractors for the quarter were Trex (TREX), Brown & Brown (BRO), CDW (CDW), Floor & Décor (FND), and Liberty Broadband (LBRDA). Trex shares declined meaningfully following their 3Q results and management’s outlook for 4Q. The company is facing headwinds from a slowing demand environment as well as an intensifying competitive environment. Brown & Brown reported another quarter of slowing organic growth and management suggested the fourth quarter will see more of the same. While growth is slowing, it is simply moderating to a more normalized level and profitability remains high, setting up the company well to continue to compound value over the long run. CDW reported improving revenue growth in 3Q, however, operating expenses grew faster in the quarter, so earnings growth was muted. Longer term, we believe that expenses will be held in check and earnings growth will pick back up.

Floor & Décor lagged the market as the difficult housing market continues to weigh on the sales of flooring. We believe the company is managing well through this cycle. Liberty Broadband, which derives nearly all its value from its ownership stake in Charter Communications, dropped as Charter reported a second straight quarter of disappointing profitability and broadband subscriber losses. Despite this, we believe the prospects for increased free cash flow provide solid valuation support for the stock.

Portfolio Activity

We added four new investments to the portfolio: Bio-Techne, ServiceTitan, A.O. Smith, and MSA Safety. Bio-Techne (TECH) is a leader in the life sciences research market with a broad portfolio of products that are used to enable discoveries of new drugs, therapeutics, and diagnostics. These products include over 6,000 proteins, 400,000 antibody types, and 2,400 diagnostic assays. Around 80% of the business is recurring revenues. We are encouraged by the new CEO’s strategy to leverage the core business and expand the company’s leadership position in protein research. Consternation surrounding the health of customer research spending gave us the opportunity to buy the stock at a discount to our estimate of intrinsic value.

ServiceTitan (TTAN) is the dominant provider of software to residential trade contractors, such as HVAC technicians, plumbers, and pest control employees. Its software helps run all aspects of their business, including dispatching, marketing, invoicing, and estimating, and the company boasts high retention rates and strong recurring revenue. We see a long runway of growth ahead as penetration among contractors has lagged other industries. Investor caution regarding near-term growth rates provided us with a buying opportunity.

A.O. Smith (AOS) designs and manufactures water heaters, boilers, and water treatment products. Around 80-85% of water heater demand is from replacement units, providing for a more consistent demand profile. A.O. Smith is the market share leader, operating within a rational oligopoly that drives high returns on invested capital. The company is well positioned to benefit from upcoming energy regulations as well as improved capital deployment from new CEO Steve Shafer. The stock traded down to an appealing valuation following weakening results in its China business as well as general negative sentiment on anything housing-related.

MSA Safety (MSA) is the dominant provider of fire fighter safety equipment, gas/flame detection equipment, and industrial protective products. The company benefits from leading market positions and strong brand recognition. Growth is driven by technological advancements and updated regulatory standards that catalyze multi-year replacement cycles. The business is conservatively managed, has low balance sheet leverage, and generates fantastic returns on invested capital. The stock is trading at an attractive valuation given the quality of the business and what we believe is the potential for growth to accelerate.

We sold two holdings, Trex (TREX) and Microchip Technology (MCHP). Shortly after we purchased Trex, their largest competitor, Azek, was acquired by a large building products company, James Hardie. Hardie offered Azek greater scale for marketing and promoting their brands, and had a reputation for being very aggressive in going after market share and distribution agreements. Trex’s third quarter earnings report suggested that margins would be lower next year as they fend off this competitive threat. Additionally, demand has started to slow, providing another headwind to margins. We concluded that the competitive environment had meaningfully changed since our purchase and that Trex’s margins and earnings power may be lower for a prolonged period, thus we decided to sell.

Microchip Technology is one of the leading analog and microcontroller companies, benefiting from long product lifecycles and diverse end markets. Microchip struggled with supply chain challenges over the past 24 months as lengthening production lead times led to customer over-ordering. The company’s founder, Steve Sanghi returned to the CEO role this past summer and has been successful in reducing excess inventory and positioning the company for a recovery in market share. We are encouraged by Steve’s action plans, but as the stock’s valuation has recovered, we elected to sell our small stake.

We trimmed our position in Amphenol (APH) during the quarter, on valuation. We brought the position size down to a level commensurate with the stock’s current risk/reward following a tremendous run of strong performance for the business and the stock.

Outlook and Positioning

As always, we continue our search for quality companies with shareholder-oriented management teams selling below our assessments of intrinsic value. We are invested in a variety of high-quality, durable businesses in a wide range of industries, selling for reasonable, or even cheap, prices. This has historically been a formula for solid, long-term performance. We don’t think that has changed. But recently, these attributes have been unpopular, as the market has favored the short-term over the long-term, the promise versus the proven, and momentum over durability. While we dedicate a considerable amount of time to understanding new technologies and using them ourselves we will not risk our investors’ hard-earned savings by abandoning a philosophy that makes rational, clear-headed sense to us, and that has worked for decades, in order to conform to something that may be fleeting, and appears to be dependent on highly optimistic scenarios. Our style of companies may temporarily be out of favor, but we believe our all-weather portfolio remains well-positioned to deliver attractive risk adjusted returns in a variety of market environments.

Respectfully,

Haruki Toyama

Andy Romanowich

Rich Eisinger
