data.title: Banco Santander: I Am Cautious As The Long-Term Risk-Reward Proposition Is Not Great
data.description:
As I've covered back in October, I wasn't particularly bullish on Banco Santander, S.A. (SAN) as the bank’s premium valuation compared to historical values and its relatively weak earnings momentum were two major factors making me cautious. Despite that, its shares have performed quite well since then and have maintained their bull run, being up by close to 28% when including dividends since then and beating the S&P 500 Index (SP500) by a wide margin during the same period.

This strong performance is mainly justified by a higher valuation multiple rather than earnings or book value growth, as the market continues to push banking valuations upwards, supported by good capital return prospects for shareholders.

Banco Santander has been no exception and is now trading at about 1.62x book value, a much higher valuation than when I last covered it (1.27x book value at the time), despite its earnings momentum being somewhat weak and its growth prospects not being particularly impressive. As shown in the next graph, Santander’s current valuation is at its peak over the last decade and has re-rated considerably in recent months, a trend that has been widespread across the European banking sector.

Despite higher valuations and positive investor sentiment toward European banks, not all banks are reporting higher earnings in recent quarters as the interest rate path has been downward in Europe since mid-2024. As Southern European banks are more exposed to rates than other European banks due to higher exposure to variable-rate loans, the earnings momentum of Banco Santander and some of its peers has not been particularly great in recent quarters, mainly due to some margin pressure in its domestic market. Indeed, the bank’s earnings on Q3 2025 were practically flat when adjusting for the hyperinflation in Argentina, not being supportive of a higher share price lately.

This relatively weak earnings momentum is also expected to have been maintained during the fourth quarter of 2025 (with earnings expected to be released in the coming days, on or about February 4th), considering that according to analysts’ estimates Santander’s revenues over the full year are expected to be around €62 billion, being slightly down compared to the previous year. Its operating earnings should increase by less than 1%, as the bank’s cost-cutting program resulted in some operating efficiencies, while the bottom-line is expected to be around €13.4 billion, up by mid-single digits compared to 2024. However, investors should note that most gains are related to the Argentina hyperinflation adjustment, while adjusted net earnings should be rather flat compared to the previous year.

For 2026, the street also seems to be somewhat pessimistic about the bank’s earnings momentum given that revenues are expected to decline a little bit to below €61.8 billion and its operating earnings should decline by 1% YoY to €35.9 billion. This shows that Santander’s business model is quite sensitive to interest rates as the vast majority of revenues are generated from net interest income and the business is still quite exposed to variable-rate loans.

This profile is not expected to change much in the near future as Santander’s business model is highly reliant on retail and commercial loans, even though new loans production has a more balanced mix between fixed and variable rates. Additionally, while other European banks have used their securities' portfolio to hedge some interest rate sensitivity to rates, as I’ve recently covered on Barclays (BCS) for instance, Santander’s bond portfolio only represents 8% of its total assets and is not a major contributor to its overall net interest income. This is another reason why Santander’s revenues have been more sensitive to rate movements in Europe than some of its peers, a trend that is likely to persist ahead.

On the other hand, the European Central Bank has stopped cutting rates back in mid-2025, which means the bank’s NII is likely to stabilize in the second half of 2026 and may, eventually, start to increase thereafter if Santander pushes for loan growth. However, this has not happened during the first nine months of 2025, as the loan book was flat at €597 billion, plus the bank has two pending deals to sell its Polish operations and buy TSB Bank from Banco Sabadell (BNDSY) in England, which should have a slightly negative effect on NII.

This means Santander’s growth prospects over the next few quarters aren’t particularly impressive, but despite that, its shares have performed quite well over the past few months, as I’ve discussed previously, as investors seem to be more focused on the bank’s capital return prospects.

This happens because Santander’s capital ratio was 13.1% at the end of last September, which is already above its internal target range of being between 12-13% over the medium term, while the sale of 49% of the Polish unit will generate additional capital around €6.4 billion and have a positive effect on Santander’s CET1 ratio of about 100 basis points. While a good part of this capital generation will be used to acquire TSB in England (around €3 billion), this still leaves plenty of excess capital to perform further share buybacks and grow its ordinary dividend ahead.

The bank’s guidance is to perform at least some €10 billion of share buybacks between 2025-26, but I think this goal is likely to be revised upwards over the coming months when Santander finalizes its M&A deals, being an important support for its share price. Given that Santander’s current market value is about €160 billion, this share buyback program represents some 6.3% of its current market value, with a larger part expected to be performed during 2026 as the bank should have done only around €3.3 billion of share buybacks during 2025. This means Santander will be more active on the capital markets during this year repurchasing its own shares, which is an important support for its share price ahead.

On top of share buybacks, the bank also makes frequent dividend distributions, with the total dividend related to 2025 earnings expected to be around €0.23 per share. This is slightly below the market estimate when I last covered Santander, as the bank’s dividend policy is directly related to earnings and there have been some downward earnings revisions recently. At its current share price, this leads to a forward dividend yield about 2.16%, which is much lower than in the recent past due to a strong share price rally and is not particularly attractive to income-oriented investors.

In my opinion, this is also a sign of some overvaluation as there are plays in the European banking sector offering much higher dividend yields and the average dividend yield across European banks is about 3.8%. While in the past Santander was an attractive income pick and offered dividend yields consistently between 4-5%, this is no longer the case as the share price increases have clearly outpaced earnings and dividend growth in recent years.

Conclusion

While I see Banco Santander as a quality bank in the European banking sector and its exposure to emerging markets usually give it better growth prospects than compared to peers, this has changed over the past few quarters due to a declining interest rate environment in Europe. Santander’s business model is highly sensitive to rates and the bank’s asset mix doesn’t help much to offset sensitivity to rates, like some of its competitors have done recently to hedge NII against falling rates. Not surprisingly, Santander’s earnings momentum has been relatively weak recently, and this profile is not expected to change much during 2026.

Despite this background, its shares continued a strong share price rally in recent months, as the market seems to be more focused on capital returns than earnings growth. This has pushed its valuation to levels not seen over the past decade, which doesn’t seem to be sustainable in the near future. While I’m not expecting its share price to be weaker ahead if the equity bull run continues over the next few months, I think the current risk-reward proposition is not particularly attractive to long-term investors. Moreover, from an income perspective its appeal is also low and, therefore, I remain cautious about SAN shares for the time being.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: VYM: Balanced Fundamentals And A Moderate Yield Is What Attracts $71B In AUM
data.description:
Investment Thesis

This article continues my coverage of the Vanguard High Dividend Yield ETF (VYM), a $71B product that's performed well this year relative to the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY). Though many analysts have declared a sentiment shift that favors the smaller, more value-oriented stocks VYM holds, this article argues that this declaration is premature and that it's not yet reasonable to expect VYM to outperform SPY. However, this article also evaluates VYM against four large-cap value peers, illustrating how its balance, both at the sector and fundamental levels, is why it's historically been a consistent performer year over year. Overall, these features make VYM a solid "hold" for investors seeking a moderate ~2.50% dividend yield, and I look forward to explaining why in further detail below. I hope you enjoy the read.

VYM Overview

One challenge with assessing VYM is that its strategy is proprietary. An Index methodology document exists, but it offers few clues surrounding the selection process beyond the basics. These include how the selection universe for the FTSE High Dividend Yield Index is the FTSE US All Cap Index, how its membership is reviewed annually, and how REITs and non-dividend-paying securities are not eligible. VYM's cheap 0.06% expense ratio and 0.01% 30-day median bid/ask spread are other noteworthy factors, but in my opinion, they don't really help us differentiate it from the 100+ other competing funds.

However, we can learn more about what the Index likely aims to accomplish by evaluating it fundamentally. To start, consider how the dividend yields of VYM's top holdings are not in descending order, with Broadcom's (AVGO) 0.81% dividend yield in the #1 spot and higher-yielding stocks like Chevron (CVX) and Philip Morris International (PM) in the #13 and #19 spots, respectively. Instead, the correlation between weight and market cap appears much stronger, which tells me that weight is likely a function of a company's size, yield, and the Index possibly considers secondary factors like liquidity and sector mix.

One reason to temper expectations about VYM's performance is because it's historically delivered about average returns. As shown below, VYM's annual returns ranked in the second or third quartile in most years between 2016 and 2025. There were some exceptions, like in 2022, when VYM's exposure to Energy stocks proved beneficial in the high-inflation environment. However, that benefit quickly reversed the following year, when inflation peaked, and growth-oriented funds started to perform better. While traders may capitalize on these short-term movements, I believe VYM's appeal comes mainly from delivering consistent, slightly better-than-average returns most of the time.

VYM Performance Summary

While SPY mostly reflects VYM's selection universe, it's not an appropriate comparator. For example, I estimate its indicated yield is more than 2x higher (2.54% vs. 1.17%, excluding fund expenses), and its $318B weighted average market cap is about $1T lower. After applying some reasonable constraints (market cap between $100-$500B, estimated yield above 2%, 10+ year track record), I found only 15 peers. Their performance statistics from January 2016 to December 2025 are summarized below, and they show how VYM's 11.32% annualized return ranks #7/16 in this group.

VYM shareholders will also appreciate how its 23.98% maximum drawdown in Q1 2020 wasn't unusual at all. In contrast, the top-performing WisdomTree U.S. Value ETF (WTV) declined by 31.43% that quarter, and its performance statistics are obviously skewed by its 77.27% "best twelve months" result in the year that followed. To avoid performance chasing, I strongly recommend evaluating WTV based on its fundamentals, and although it's not the subject of today's article, I can say that it scores lower than VYM in my fundamentals-driven rankings system as of January 24, 2026.

Still, it's hard to ignore the iShares Core Dividend Growth ETF (DGRO), the Vanguard Mega Cap Value ETF (MGV), and the Vanguard Value ETF (VTV). All three were better than VYM on annualized returns, maximum drawdowns, worst twelve months, and risk-adjusted returns (Sharpe and Sortino ratios), and I find the differences in estimated yields to be negligible (~0.40%). For those not wanting to compromise yield, I will also look at the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) with the understanding that its Index is scheduled to reconstitute on March 23, 2026. As discussed here, I anticipate a more fundamentally balanced portfolio will emerge on that date, which should hopefully get it back on track from a total returns perspective this year.

VYM Analysis

VYM Sector Allocations vs. Peer Group

A key reason why VYM delivers consistently solid annual returns is because it's well-balanced at the sector level. As shown below, Financials is its largest sector at 22.27%, but it also has 15.56% allocated to Technology, and that helps ensure at least reasonable participation in bull markets. In contrast, SCHD's Index is lighter on Technology (8%) and heavier on Energy (21%) and Consumer Staples (18%), and that combination hasn't worked well in the strong bull market from 2023 to 2025.

Though VYM's Index is subject to change at each reconstitution, its portfolio turnover rate ranged from just 8-13% for the last five fiscal years. When you think about all that's transpired over this period, these rates are incredibly low and suggest the Index is designed not to panic. In my view, this contributes to its consistent returns profile, whereas higher turnover ETFs like SCHD have much more sensitive inputs and, thus, are expected to either substantially outperform or underperform in any given year. That's why I place so much emphasis on SCHD's annual reconstitutions and not so much on VYM's.

VYM Fundamental Analysis: Top 20 Holdings

The following table highlights selected fundamental statistics for VYM's top 20 holdings, which comprise 40.09% of its portfolio. What's interesting is that these statistics do not definitively conclude that VYM is better than DGRO, MGV, or VTV in its current state. It depends on what factors you value, so let's go through them one by one to understand the tradeoffs.

Market Risk

VYM's five-year portfolio beta is 0.83, which is almost identical to DGRO, MGV, and VTV. Further down the market risk line is SCHD at 0.66, while SPY's is substantially higher at 1.14. You're probably used to seeing SPY's beta pegged to 1.00, which is standard practice for data providers. However, this weighted average calculation illustrates just how much riskier core funds have become as high-risk stocks like Nvidia (NVDA), with a five-year beta of 2.31, have cemented their leadership. This fact alone may be reason enough for some investors to reduce their risk, and large-cap value ETFs in general are a good way to do so.

Growth

VYM's components have increased their sales and earnings per share by about 5% per year over the last three years, with analysts expecting 9.77% more earnings growth over the next twelve months. DGRO, MGV, and VTV have similar growth rates, which is why they're reasonable alternatives, while SCHD's components actually saw their earnings decline by an annualized 7.38% over the last three years. Weak or negative growth is a persistent problem for SCHD lately, so it's no surprise to see its returns were only 4.20% annualized from 2023 to 2025, with nearly all of this sourced to dividends rather than share price appreciation.

VYM's growth rates are competitive within this peer group, but we shouldn't ignore how far apart they are from SPY's. Since SPY's components increased EPS by 3x more over the last three years (15.27% vs. 4.95%), it's important to call out the potential for a significant tradeoff on total returns. Consider that even if the market's lofty growth expectations aren't met, VYM will need a meaningful valuation compression, likely in the tech sector, to close the gap and start outperforming SPY. DGRO, MGV, and VTV face a similar challenge.

Valuation

VYM currently trades at 20.49x trailing earnings and 17.27x forward earnings based on data from January 24, 2026. That's about 1-2 points cheaper than DGRO and MGV, on par with VTV, and about two points more than SCHD. This demonstrates the kind of balance VYM achieves and what so many investors appreciate, even if they aren't tuned in to these specifics. It's obviously not the cheapest fund in its category, but when factoring in its growth rates, it's arguably the best of the five.

Dividends

VYM's turnover rates are low, which means that most of its reported dividend growth relates to the dividend growth rates of its underlying holdings. In contrast, ETFs like SCHD can synthetically create more dividend growth by systematically rebalancing into high-yielding stocks. From a DGI perspective, this is a smart way to take advantage of an ETF's open-ended structure, which allows for "in-kind" transactions to occur with no tax consequences. However, the downside is that it could mask real fundamental issues with an ETF's underlying holdings. For example, the earnings for SCHD's holdings decreased by an annualized 7.38% over the last three years, yet those same holdings increased their dividends by an annualized 6.76%. It doesn't take much of an analyst to realize that this gap is unsustainable - the expected behavior is for markets to limit share price appreciation, which is exactly what happened with SCHD.

Fortunately, I believe VYM is on solid ground when it comes to dividend yield, dividend consistency, dividend safety, and dividend growth. As shown below, its 2.48% estimated yield (2.54% Index yield less 0.06% expense ratio) is a bit better than average, its 4.95% three-year historical EPS growth rate supports adequate dividend growth without stretching the payout ratio too much, and the ETF itself is on an impressive 15-year dividend growth streak.

That long streak itself doesn't necessarily mean dividend growth is key to the selection process. Keep in mind that SPY is also on a 16-year dividend growth streak, and all it represents is 500 of the largest U.S. stocks by market cap. Instead, the likely reason for the streak is that its holdings are on good financial footing, and in its own way, VYM does that reasonably well.

Quality

We can measure if an ETF is on good financial footing by calculating the same profitability statistics you've probably come across using stock screeners. All that's required is to merge stock data and fund weightings while applying some soft constraints to limit the influence of outliers, and after doing this, I found that VYM ranks about average on several measures compared to its large-cap value ETF peers, including:

  • Asset Turnover: 0.68 (#86/149)
  • EBIT Margin: 22.59% (#65/149)
  • Net Margin: 17.83% (#52/149)
  • ROA: 6.83% (#94/149)
  • ROE: 21.97% (#68/149)
  • ROTC: 11.17% (#85/149)

There's no universal measure of quality, but VYM scoring close to average on numerous profitability measures aligns well with what we've observed with its performance. My ranking system, which aggregates these statistics while emphasizing operational efficiency (EBIT margins) and capital efficiency (return on total capital), suggests DGRO is a bit better on quality. However, SPY's 8.48/10 quality composite score is miles ahead of all five large-cap value funds and reminds us that the largest companies also happen to be the most profitable. As a result, I don't advise betting against them.

Momentum and Sentiment

I mentioned earlier how large-cap value ETFs have done well so far this year. As shown below, VYM is up 3.55%, while SCHD, the most value-oriented fund discussed today, is up 7.11%. Given how SPY's gain is rather modest at 1.99%, many are wondering whether the tide has finally shifted away from growth and into value.

I'm encouraged by these results, but I also remain skeptical, mainly because SPY's holdings are still delivering solid earnings surprises. My S&P 500 Index EPS Surprise Tracker notes how the Index is on an impressive 11-quarter streak of above-average earnings surprises, and the Index's weighted surprise last quarter was close to 9% compared to about 6% for VYM. To be sure, sentiment can change quickly, but I prefer to wait for hard numbers to come in this quarter before assessing if value stocks really are ready to take over.

Investment Recommendation

I think VYM is in pretty good shape fundamentally. Its estimated dividend yield is 2.48%, which is about 0.40% more than close peers like DGRO, MGV, and VTV. While this yield is well below that of SCHD's, the upside is a stronger earnings growth rate that supports sustained and organic dividend growth and a better-balanced portfolio all around, both from a fundamental and sector allocation perspective. This balance is why VYM consistently delivers close-to-average returns each year compared to its category peers, and that's the most important thing readers should know about the fund.

Over the long term, I prefer higher quality over cheaper valuations, so I lean towards DGRO over VYM, and I think that's supported by its superior total and risk-adjusted returns. However, VYM is still a good choice for those aiming for consistency over trying to beat the market using a more active approach, so for those reasons, it deserves a solid "hold" rating. Thank you for reading, and I look forward to your comments below.

data.title: SP500: Sell The Highs, All Risk Events Delayed To February
data.description:
The All-time high resistance

The S&P 500 (SP500) is at the all-time high resistance that has been building since October 2025 - so that's four months of flat performance. In my opinion, this reflects a topping pattern. The price tried to breach the resistance on several occasions, unsuccessfully.

The key support level is the rising 100dma (red line). Similarly, there were several attempts to breach the support level, also unsuccessfully. The 100dma support is also the bottom of the rising regression channel since the April 2025 bottom.

Thus, we have a narrowing pattern or flag where the S&P 500 is unable to break to the new highs, but at the same time, it's holding the uptrend support.

Obviously, this situation will resolve itself either with 1) the breakout to the new all-time highs to the 7500 level at the top of the regression channel or 2) the 100dma breakdown to the next support level at 6500 - which could mark the beginning of the major correction and possibly even a recessionary bear market with the bubble burst.

The breakdown is more likely

Technically, we can simply follow the chart and see what happens, where the proper strategy is to buy the breakout or sell the breakdown - no questions asked.

However, fundamentally, a breakdown is more likely.

First of all, we are not in a recession, although many soft-data indicators like consumer and business confidence are at recession levels. Yet, we are not trading based on recession fears at the moment.

Second, inflation is not a problem - officially. The data is pointing to modestly elevated inflation, which is slightly falling. However, unofficially, inflation is a major risk due to the currency debasement and Sell America themes, as we will explain in this article.

Third, credit risk is very low, based on very tight credit spreads - so this is also positive for the stock market.

Altogether, the macro situation appears to be very benign and supportive of the breakout scenario. However, the macro risks are rising, and it seems like most of these risks are converging over the next few weeks. Thus, fundamentally, the breakdown over the near term is more likely.

Given the benign macro environment and the fact that the S&P 500 is near the all-time highs, the VIX (VIX) should be very low in the 12-13 area. However, the VIX is at 16, which suggests that the macro risk is elevated.

The risk list

The risk list is getting longer by the day, and the probability of the negative outcome is very high for each risk - and these are all imminent events.

The Sell America trade - Geopolitics - Iran

Geopolitically, we are in the new world order, not simple de-globalization. De-globalization implies something controlled and peaceful. The formation of the new world order is something hostile. The Sell America trade is the outcome of this process, manifested with a falling US Dollar (DXY) and higher US yields (US10Y), and in some situations, higher oil prices (USO).

An imminent event is a likely US strike on Iran, which could happen imminently, as the US armada already arrived in the Gulf. This could cause the spike in the price of oil - and the increase in volatility is negative for the stock market.

In addition, the aftermath of the Greenland situation is likely to continue to support the Sell America trade, which is negative for the stock market.

An imminent government shutdown

The US government is likely to go into another shutdown after January 30th, as the Democrats in the Senate seem unlikely to support the funding for ICE after the unfortunate situation in Minneapolis over the weekend.

This even also supports a weakening US Dollar and higher yields, consistent with the Sell America trade.

The debasement trade - the new Fed Chair announcement

President Trump is also likely to announce the new Fed Chair imminently. If the new Fed Chair lacks market credibility, the market could also push the US Dollar lower and the US long-term rates higher due to the currency debasement fears - this is also consistent with the Sell America trade and negative for the stock market.

The SCOTUS decision on tariffs

The SCOUTS has delayed the decision on the legality of Trump tariffs to at least late February. Most expected the decision to come in January, but likely due to Trump's trip to Davos, the decision has been delayed.

If SCOTUS strikes down the Trump tariffs, the Trump economic plan essentially collapses, but Trump is likely to reimplement the tariffs using other statutes and possibly with other, more aggressive policies, which could accelerate the Sell America trade.

The unfolding Yen carry trade blow-up

Japanese JGB yields have been rising, and the Japanese Yen has started to spike, which means that this situation could cause the unwind of the yen carry trade or forced selling by the hedge funds who borrow in Yen to fund their positions.

In addition, Japanese investors could start selling US Treasuries to invest in the domestic bonds as the yield differential narrows, which could also result in higher US yields.

The potential AI bubble burst with the earnings season

The S&P 500 is a bubble trading at a Shiller PE ratio above 40 because the mega caps are overvalued due to the AI theme. The AI bubble could burst imminently during the current earnings season due to these issues: the questions about ROI in AI capex, the questions about AI chip depreciation, and the issue of debt financing and the circular investing themes. Even if the mega-caps beat the earnings estimates, any indication that these issues could become a problem could burst the bubble.

Implications

We are in a very unusual situation where many serious macro risks are converging over the next few weeks, while the S&P 500 is trading near the all-time high - at a bubble-like valuation.

It's always difficult to predict a market crash, but the current situation could produce one.

It seems like the channel to the stock market crash is through the depreciating US Dollar and higher US yields. The US dollar has started weakening in the aftermath of the Greenland situation. The 10Y yields also rose but retreated and remain stable - so this is the metric to follow.

The only indicator of the current situation is Gold (GLD) - an ultimate hedge against macro volatility related to a falling US Dollar, and the warning about the unfolding situation. Gold just breached the 5000 level.

Thus, in my opinion, the S&P 500 is likely to have a breakdown over the next few weeks as the situation unfolds, and my rating is a Strong Sell.
